coredata.dc:title: Size controlled synthesis and fluorescence quenching behavior of N-CQDs based on molecular sieves
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Zhi
Yuan, Dongdong
Lu, Qinggeng
Deng, Huiping
Shi, Jun
Xie, Xiaofeng
coredata.dc:description: Tunable photoluminescence (PL) of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) strongly depends on the characteristics of particle size and edge chemistry. In this work, we report a very facile and effective method to separate nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) by molecular sieves with different pore sizes through physical-chemical adsorption method. The N-CQDs-3.7 with target sizes of 1–3 nm and N-CQDs-8 with target sizes of 4–7 nm are rapidly obtained through the screening of MCM-41 and SBA-15, respectively. The microstructure and PL performance of the separated N-CQDs are explored by transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, Raman scattering spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and PL spectroscopy. The two types of N-CQDs exhibit different pH responses and fluorescence quenching behaviors to aniline compounds and benzoic acid (BA). The N-CQDs-8 emits a stable green fluorescence at pH = 2–7, and exhibits an obvious PL quenching behavior to alkaline aniline compounds, especially the aromatic compounds with di-amine. On the opposite, the N-CQDs-3.7 illuminates a stable blue fluorescence at pH = 7–10, and displays a significant PL quenching for the acidic BA, representing a good linear relationship and high sensitivity. Meanwhile, the different pH responses and PL quenching performances are also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Separation
Size effect
Fluorescent performance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL and adsorption performances of N-CQDs-8 to aniline
Sensing performance of N-CQDs-3.7 to BA
Fluorescence response of the N-CQDs-8 to aniline
Fluorescence response of the N-CQDs-3.7 to BA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of separation process of N-CQDs by molecular sieves with different pore sizes.
Optical performance of the N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 specimens. (a) UV–Vis spectra; (b) and (c) PLE and PL spectra of the N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 specimens, respectively; (d) Raman spectra.

Microstructure of N-CQDs-3.7 (a, b) and N-CQDs-8 (c, d). (a, c) TEM images; (b, d) AFM images. The insets in (a, c) are the corresponding particle size distributions, and those in (b, d) are the height-profiles.
XPS spectra of the N-CQDs-3.7 and N-CQDs-8. (a) Survey spectra; (b–d) C 1s, O 1s, N 1s spectra, respectively.
pH dependence of luminescence performance of the N-CQDs. (a) Fluorescence pictures; (b) PL spectra.
Fluorescence performance of the N-CQDs to aniline. (a) PL spectra of N-CQDs-8 to aniline; (b) Linear response of N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 to different concentrations of aniline.
TG curves of the MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 after absorption of aniline gas. (a) Pictures of the MCM-41 with and without N-CQDs-8 under UV light. (b) TG curves.

Fluorescence quenching performance of N-CQD-3.7 toward BA. (a) PL responses of N-CQDs-3.7 to different concentrations of BA; (b) Linear relationship of PL intensity with the concentration of BA; (d) Interference tests.
PL spectra of N-CQDs-8 toward aniline, oPD, mPD and pPD in (a) and benzene, toluene and o-xylene in (b) with the same concentration of 25 mM.
Illustration of the PL quenching behavior of N-CQDs to aniline and BA.
Determination results of BA in sewage.

head.ce:title: Size controlled synthesis and fluorescence quenching behavior of N-CQDs based on molecular sieves

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai institute of pollution control and ecological security, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: POWERCHINA Group Environmental Engineering Company, Hangzhou, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: POWERCHINA group environmental engineering company, Hangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tunable photoluminescence (PL) of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) strongly depends on the characteristics of particle size and edge chemistry. In this work, we report a very facile and effective method to separate nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) by molecular sieves with different pore sizes through physical-chemical adsorption method. The N-CQDs-3.7 with target sizes of 1–3 nm and N-CQDs-8 with target sizes of 4–7 nm are rapidly obtained through the screening of MCM-41 and SBA-15, respectively. The microstructure and PL performance of the separated N-CQDs are explored by transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, Raman scattering spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and PL spectroscopy. The two types of N-CQDs exhibit different pH responses and fluorescence quenching behaviors to aniline compounds and benzoic acid (BA). The N-CQDs-8 emits a stable green fluorescence at pH = 2–7, and exhibits an obvious PL quenching behavior to alkaline aniline compounds, especially the aromatic compounds with di-amine. On the opposite, the N-CQDs-3.7 illuminates a stable blue fluorescence at pH = 7–10, and displays a significant PL quenching for the acidic BA, representing a good linear relationship and high sensitivity. Meanwhile, the different pH responses and PL quenching performances are also discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received extensive attention as a new type of carbon-based photoluminescence (PL) semiconductor due to the powerful quantum confinement effect [1]. Many special chemical and physical properties of CQDs are found, such as high luminesce, long-term chemical stability, low photobleaching [2,3], biocompatibility [4], and low toxicity, exhibiting many potential applications in sensing [5,6], bioimaging, nanomedicine [7,8], and energy storage [9]. As one of the most important characteristics, the optical performance of CQDs is widely investigated, showing a strong tunability of size-dependent transition and surface chemistry [10]. Previous works have reported a variety of methods to adjust PL performance of CQDs, including surface modification [11,12], doping [13,14], dimensional control [15,16]. Peng et al. [17] prepared three kinds of CQDs with different sizes of 1–4, 4–8, and 7–11 nm via varying the reaction temperature, and finally obtained different PL responses from blue to green, and then to yellow. Kumar et al. [18] reported the photoluminescence performance of an amino-functionalized graphene quantum dot (GQD), showing an obvious dependence of amino-edge effect. The PL of CQDs is strongly related to the intrinsic state of sp2 hybridized crystalline structure and the extrinsic state associated with surface chemistry. Meanwhile, the charge transfer from the intrinsic to the extrinsic states also influence the PL performance [19]. However, the effects of the defect modes and particle size on the PL performance are still unclear due to the difficult separation and purification of CQDs.

The present separation methods are hard to purify the CQDs due to the complicated size distribution, surface and edge structure, which limit the understanding of PL response mechanisms [20]. General methods to separate CQDs are dialysis filter [21,22], gradient ultracentrifugation [23], and size exclusion chromatography, which usually contain impurities and have unsatisfactory separation effect. Fan et al. [24] successfully separated GQDs using column chromatography on silica gel to obtain the particles with sizes of 1.9, 2.4, 3.0 and 3.9 nm. However, the dominate problems of this technology are low yield, time consuming, and complex operation. Moreover, the doping of heteroatoms shall make the separation process more complicated. Therefore, an efficient, feasible and simple method to separate CQDs is urgently required to control the size and heterogeneous groups on the surface of CQDs for the target applications.

Aromatic compounds, such as aniline and benzoic acid (BA), are toxic, and generally from printing and dyeing wastewater, pulp and paper industries, and from waste gas of petrochemical and coal industries [25]. However, the demand for aromatic chemicals shows a clear upward trend with the development of fine chemicals. Thus, it is very important to establish a simple, fast and effective technology to detect and remove the anilines and their derivatives. Recently, CQDs are found to interact with aniline derivatives, and produce luminescence quenching behavior [26], which might be used to detect the aniline derivatives. As a representative of silicon-based mesoporous materials, molecular sieves possess homogeneous channel structure and pore size distribution, and thereafter, have excellent properties, such as adjustable pore size, high specific surface area, and large adsorption capacity [27]. More importantly, molecular sieves exhibit new adsorption and catalysis performances after regulated by post-functionalization modification. In this work, we develop an effective method to tune the sizes and surface functional groups of CQDs through molecular sieve using physical-chemical adsorption method. Since the special pore structure of molecular sieve is very suitable to quickly and effectively separate CQDs with sizes of 0–10 nm, different sizes of CQDs can be obtained by using molecular sieves with different mesoporous structures. Interesting, the screened CQDs with different particle sizes and surface chemistry present different PL performances to the aniline and BA, which are not discovered in the as-prepared CQDs. Meanwhile, the CQDs endows the mesoporous materials a new adsorption performance to aniline compounds through surface modification. The combination of molecular sieves with CQDs generates special physicochemical properties, and widens the applications of the mesoporous materials. The proposed method opens a new direction in the development of fine-controlling CQDs with special PL performances.

The chemicals of sodium hydroxide (NaOH), citric acid (CA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), aniline, O-diphenylamine (oPD), M-diphenylamine (mPD) and P-diphenylamine (pPD), benzene, toluene and o-xylene were purchased from Alighting Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Benzoic acid (BA), sodium fluoride (NaF), zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) and Co(NO3)2 were purchased from Sinopharmaceuticals Group Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. Commercial SBA-15 and MCM-41 molecular sieves were purchased from Molecular Sieves Catalytic Chemical Co. LTD.

CA (52 mg) and mPD (80 mg) were dissolved into absolute ethanol (35 mL), then transferred into a 40 mL stainless autoclave after by ultrasonic stirring of 30 min. The mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 2 h. The synthesized N-CQDs were collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and then ultrasonic cleaned with ethanol for 3 times.

Two different types of molecular sieves were used to separate the N-CQDs by the MCM-41 and SBA-15 with average pore sizes of 3.7 and 8 nm, respectively. The separation process is shown in Fig. 1 . Specifically, 0.2 g of SBA-15 zeolite was placed in a 10 mL syringe, and then the N-CQDs solution (3 mL) was filled into the upper side of the syringe, then pushed by an external force to control the propulsion rate of 3 mL min−1. Extrusion operations were repeated by 3 times to obtain N-CQDs solution with the sizes from 0 to 8 nm (N-CQDs-8). Correspondingly, the N-CQDs separated by MCM-41 molecular sieve were denoted as N-CQDs-3.7.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope. Raman scattering spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 532 nm laser. The UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerKinElmer, USA). The morphology of the N-CQDs was observed by atomic force microscope (AFM, Shimadzu SPM9700) operating at a semi-tapping mode. The PL spectroscopy was recorded using a Shimadzu RF5301 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a ThermoFisher-250XI X-ray electron spectrometer with focused monochromatized Al Kα radiation. The thermogravimetry (TG) experiments were carried out on PekinElmer Pyris 1 TGA with a heating rate of 0.1o min−1 at 25−600 °C under N2 protection.
The PL spectra of the as-prepared N-CQDs at different pH values were determined at 440 nm for N-CQDs-8 and 340 nm for N-CQDs-3.7, where solution pH was adjusted by 0.1 M HNO3 and 0.1 M NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and adsorption performances of N-CQDs-8 to aniline
ce:section.ce:para: Aniline (3.0 mL) was dissolved in distilled water (100 mL) as a starting solution, then diluted to a certain concentration by distilled water. The PL spectra of the aniline solution with different concentrations were determined by N-CQDs-3.7 and N-CQDs-8. Similarly, the PL spectra of the N-CQDs-8 to oPD, mPD and pPD, benzene, toluene and o-xylene with the concentration of 25 mM were determined at the same condition. The adsorption of MCM-41 molecule sieve with N-CQDs-8 to aniline gas was carried out in a well-closed container with a volume of 1 L, where the MCM-41 molecule sieve (0.2 g) and aniline (5, 30 μL) were placed on two sides of a glass slide for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of N-CQDs-3.7 to BA
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of N-CQDs-3.7 to BA were measured at 340 nm. The starting concentration of BA was 20 mM. When measuring the PL spectra of the interferences, the concentration of the interfering substances, involving the common metal ions (Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+) and anions (NO3 −, S2−, Cl−, SO4 2−) were 200 nM, respectively. The wastewater from Shanghai Environmental Monitoring Station was diluted 100-fold with distilled water without any other pretreatment.

The fluorescence pictures of the two N-CQDs solution separated by MCM-41 and SBA-15 under 365 nm UV light irradiation are given in the insets in Fig. 2 a, where the N-CQDs-3.7 shows blue light and N-CQDs-8 emits green light. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N-CQDs-8 (Fig. 2a) shows two absorption bands at 340 and 440 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to n→π* transition of CN [28] and CO domains [29–31], respectively. However, no absorption peak is found in the N-CQDs-3.7 specimen. The fluorescence excitation (PLE) spectra of N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 show the emission peaks at 440 and 340 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b and c. Correspondingly, the PL peaks of the two specimens are 510 and 420 nm, respectively, and both exhibit a PLE independent wavelength. Combined with the UV–Vis spectra, the red shift of the PL peak of the N-CQDs-8 is strongly related to the CO functional group. The Raman spectra of the two N-CQDs show obvious disordered (D) and crystalline (G) bands at 1353 and 1571 cm−1 (Fig. 2d), respectively [32,33]. The fitting results of N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 show the intensity ratio (ID/IG) is 0.82 and 0.92, respectively, which are lower than that of reported results (approx.1.05) [34,35], indicating a relatively higher crystallinity structure. Generally, the better crystalline of CQDs means a repairing conjugated sp2 surface, and shall result in an enhanced PL intensity [36]. The smaller intensity ratio (ID/IG) of N-CQDs-8 implies a better crystalline structure than the N-CQDs-3.7, and thereafter a strong PL emission.

Fig. 3 a gives the TEM image of the N-CQDs-3.7, showing the particle sizes focus on 2−3 nm, and size distribution obtained from the statistic result of TEM images ranges in 1–5 nm, as shown in the inset in Fig. 3a. The AFM image of N-CQDs-3.7 (Fig. 3b) presents lots of fine spherical particles with thicknesses of 0.4–1.5 nm (1−4 graphene layers). A few larger particles of 3–4 nm thick are also observed, as indicated by arrows in Fig. 3b, which might come from the particles aggregation. The TEM image of the N-CQDs-8 (in Fig. 3c) shows the particle sizes are in the range of 1–8 nm (inset in Fig. 3c), centering at 6−7 nm. The AFM image in Fig. 3d gives the morphology of the granular particles, and the topographic heights are in the range of 1–4.0 nm corresponding to the thickness of 2–12 graphene layers. Compared Fig. 3b with 3 d, N-CQDs-8 is composed of larger and thicker particles than N-CQDs-3.7.

The XPS spectrum is used to analyze the elemental compositions and chemical states of the separated N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey spectra of the two N-CQDs both show C 1s at 284.6 eV, O 1s at 531.3 eV, and N 1s at 399.6 eV, where the intensity of N 1s in the N-CQDs-3.7 is much lower than that of the N-CQDs-8. Correspondingly, the atomic contents of the N atoms in the N-CQDs-8 and N-CQDs-3.7 are 8.8% and 3.1% (at.%), respectively. The high resolution of C 1s of the N-CQDs-3.7 (Fig. 4b) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.4, 286.0 and 288.3 eV, which are assigned to be C–C/CC, C–O–C and CO, respectively, while that of the N-CQDs-8 contains one more peak at 284.8 eV, originating from C–N group [37]. The C–C/CC peak at 284.4 eV corresponds to graphite-like sp2 C [38]. Compared with the N-CQDs-8, the N-CQDs-3.7 is mostly composed of the sp2 C, while other chemical structures of the C–N, C–O–C and CO are much less. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) is fitted into three peaks at 531.1, 532.4, and 532.8 eV, which are assigned to be CO, C–O and C–OH, respectively. Correspondingly, the N-CQDs-8 contains more CO and C–OH chemical groups, meaning that a large number of –COOH or C–OH groups exist at the edges. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) is separated into two peaks, namely pyridinic N at 398.8 eV and pyrrolic N at 400.4 eV, where the pyridinic N occupies a large fraction in both specimens. These observations agree with the result of UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2), where N-CQDs-8 contains adsorption peaks of CN and CO chemical groups.

Fig. 5 a shows the luminescence pictures of the N-CQDs-3.7 and N-CQDs-8 with pH dependence under UV light at 365 nm, where the N-CQDs-3.7 emits strong blue-green light at pH = 6–10. However, the N-CQDs-8 emits a strong green luminescence under acidic pH = 1–9, but a weak luminescence or no luminescence at pH < 9. Specifically, the fluorescent strength of the N-CQDs-3.7 gradually increases with the increase of pH up to 7, then starts to decrease with the further increase of pH (Fig. 5b). At pH = 6−9, the N-CQDs-3.7 remains a relatively stable fluorescent strength within an error of 5%. On the opposite, the N-CQDs-8 retains a strong fluorescent stability at pH = 2–6 (with an error less than 5%), and shows a significant fluorescent quenching at pH > 10. Thus, the N-CQDs-8 emits strong fluorescent in acidic (pH = 2−6) and N-CQDs-3.7 delivers a stable PL response at weak alkaline (pH = 6−9) solution, respectively. Commonly, the CQDs emit fluorescence at natural and basic solution. For example, Yuan et al. [39] reported that the fluorescence of GQDs was strong under alkaline solution and almost completely quenched under acidic condition. However, some fluorescent sensing methods of CQDs are required in acidic or extremely harsh environments, such as heavy metals in the polluted wastewater or optical bioimaging in vivo [40,41]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an acidophilic CQD to broaden their application fields. However, N-CQDs-8 exhibits a stable tolerance of fluorescent in acidic solution, which is strongly correlated with the presence of carboxylic functional group [36]. As evidenced in XPS results (Fig. 4c), N-CQDs-8 contains lots of carboxylic (COOH) group, which can conjugate to the sp2 structure to lower the band energy by intermediate valence state (n→π*), leading to a red-shift of fluorescence wavelength and a strong PL response in acid environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N-CQDs-8 to aniline
ce:section.ce:para:
By utilizing the defferent pH response of the separated N-CQDs, the fluorescence performances of the N-CQDs toward aniline and BA were tested, and concentration-dependent fluorescence response curves are shown in Fig. 6 a and b. The N-CQDs-3.7 shows a slight PL quenching, then remains a stable PL intensity when the concentration of aniline is higher than 40 mM, as given in Fig. 6b. However, the PL intensity of N-CQDs-8 exhibits a dual-linear relationship within the concentration of 0–60 mM and 60–120 mM, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6b. The regression equation of N-CQDs-8 to aniline at the first range is:
F 1
F 0
= 0.970 − 0.0116
C
(
m M
)
, and the coefficients of determination (R2) is 0.996, where the F0 is the initial PL intensity of N-CQDs-8 solution, and the F1 is the PL intensity of the mixture after the addition of aniline. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 1.8 mM.

Since the N-CQDs-8 shows a strong interaction toward aniline, the adsorption experiments of the N-CQDs-8 to aniline gas were carried out. After separation of N-CQDs-3.7, the surface of MCM-41 molecule sieve adsorbs the N-CQDs-8, which emits green fluorescence under the UV light, as presented in Fig. 7 a. The TG curves of the MCM-41 molecule sieves with N-CQDs-8 after absorption of aniline are shown in Fig. 7b. The weight loss to the point I (Fig. 7b) within 70 °C is the loss of the residual water absorbed on the surface of the sample, as shown in MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 and the one after absorption of aniline. While the MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 after absorption of aniline exhibits the second stage of weight loss at 70–180 °C (Point II in Fig. 7b) due to the evaporation of aniline [42,43]. The weight loss of the MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 are 6% and 27% after absorption of aniline of 5 and 30 μL, respectively. This result indicates that the MCM-41 after surface modified by N-CQDs-8 exhibits a new function to adsorb harmful aniline gas. When the specimen of MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 absorbed aniline was calcined at 200 °C for 2 h, the TG curve shows a similar shape to the original one of MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8, and no second weigh loss occurs. The calcination results imply that MCM-41 with N-CQDs-8 has a good recovery performance for the second round absorption of aniline compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N-CQDs-3.7 to BA
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence responses of the N-CQDs-3.7 to different concentrations of BA are presented in Fig. 8 a, where the PL intensity is sensitive to the concentration of BA. A dual-linear relationship is observed in the range of 0–20 nM and 20–120 nM, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8b. The regression equation of N-CQDs-3.7 to BA at the first range is:
F 1
F 0
= 0.953 − 0.0280
C
(
n M
)
, and the R2 is 0.966, where the F0 is the initial PL intensity of N-CQDs-3.7 solution, and F1 or F2 is the PL intensity of the mixture after the addition of BA. The LOD is estimated to be 0.7 nM. On the opposite, the N-CQDs-8 displays a weaker PL quenching behavior than the N-CQDs-3.7, as shown in Fig. 8b. When detecting residual BA in wastewater, the interference of common metal cations and anions are taken into account, such as Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, F−, NO3 −, S2−, Cl−, SO4 2−. As shown in Fig. 8c, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs-3.7 can significantly be quenched by BA, and the influences of other interferences present a relative error of 0.3–8%, and thereafter, the negative effect can be ignored. In order to prove the feasibility of sensing performance, BA in the sewage after diluted by 100 times is determined by standard addition method, and the results are shown in Table 1 . The recovery of BA is 90.8%–108.4%. Therefore, this method shows a potential application in a rapid detection of BA in polluted wastewater.

The separated N-CQDs exhibit totally different optical performance of pH response and quenching behavior for the two types of aromatic compounds. Specifically, the N-CQDs-3.7 shows a strong PL in weak basic solution and a quenching behavior to BA, while the N-CQDs-8 remains a stable PL in acidic solution and a quenching phenomenon to basic aniline. In order to find out the reasons of the PL quenching of the N-CQDs-8 to aniline, the other homologous molecules with aromatic ring and amino structure, such as oPD, mPD and pPD, benzene, toluene and o-xylene with the same concentration of 25 mM, on the PL response are also investigated, as shown in Fig. 9 . Notably, the N-CQDs-8 exhibits a significantly quenching effect by all the molecules containing amine-functional group, including aniline, oPD, mPD, and pPD, especially the mPD and pPD, as shown in Fig. 9a. Additionally, the other neutral phenyl compounds, such as benzene, toluene and o-xylene, show a weak quenching effect, as given in Fig. 9b. These observations imply that the quenching effect of the N-CQDs-8 originates from the base-amine group, and the more amine groups, and lower fluorescence intensity. In other words, the derivatives with di-amino groups are more effective than the benzene without amino group and with one amino group. Thus, the amino group plays an important role in PL quenching by the photoinduced energy transfer (PET) process via the strong coordination bonds between phosphors and receptors [44]. Moreover, O-amino group of the oPD presents a relative weak quenching effect due to the steric hindrance compared with the mPD and pPD.

Integrated with the pH response curves of the N-CDQs-3.7 and N-CQDs-8, acid-base binding reactions are reasonably drawn out, as shown in Fig. 10 . After separation of N-CQDs by molecule sieve, the original N-CQDs are screened by different sizes, together with different surface chemistry. From the analysis results of XPS spectra (in Fig. 4b), the N-CQDs-8 is composed of many oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxylic (COOH) and hydroxyl (C–OH) groups. These chemical groups are favourable to transfer photoinduced electrons exited by the UV from the N-CQDs to the adsorbents of benzene ring and aniline compounds through the bonding of carboxyl and amino groups, resulting in PL quenching. Correspondingly, a large number of amino-groups result in a larger quenching effect by the acid-base binding, as shown in Fig. 10a and b. On the opposite, the N-CQDs-3.7 is mainly composed of sp2 aromatic structure with C–O–C defect and C–OH edge group, as given in Fig. 4b and c. Correspondingly, the hydroxyl groups of N-CQDs-3.7 absorb the carboxyl group of BA to produce the PET, and PL is quenched, as illustrated in Fig. 10a and c.

Furthermore, these oxygen-containing functional groups, especially the COOH group, endow the N-CQDs-8 a strong PL in the acidic solution, and the intrinsic sp2 rings in the N-CQDs-3.7 generate blue fluorescence in neutral or alkaline solution. Additionally, the edge groups of carboxylic groups, hydroxyl, and pyridine groups can provide intermediate valence states to lower the band energy, and hence, generate red-shift from blue fluorescence to green fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. 10d and e. Hence, the sieving characteristics of molecular sieves create an opportunity to discover the special performances of N-CQDs, including fine-controlled size, surface chemistry regulation, PL performance in different pH environments, and selective absorption. Meanwhile, the surface modified molecular sieves by N-CQDs obtain strong absorption performances to different aniline compounds.

In this work, the N-CQDs can be separated effectively into two different particle sizes by using two types of molecular sieves, and their fluorescence performances display different pH responses and quenching effects to aromatic compounds. The N-CQDs-8 sieved by SAB-15 presents a particle size of 4–7 nm, and emits a stable green fluorescence at pH = 2−7, which exhibits an obvious PL quenching behavior to the alkaline aniline compounds, especially the aromatic compounds with di-amines. Conversely, the N-CQDs-3.7 screened by MCM-41 exhibits a particle size of 1–3 nm, and emits a stable blue fluorescence at pH = 6−9, showing a significant PL quenching for the acidic BA. The surface chemistry analysis indicates that the N-CQDs-8 contains carboxylic, hydroxyl, and pyridine groups, which provide intermediate valence states to lower the band energy and induce the acidic fluorescence response. The proposed separation method of CQDs by molecule sieve opens a new direction for adjusting CQDs with different particle sizes and surface chemistry, and the separated CQDs present potential applications in the detection and absorption of harmful aromatic pollutants.

Zhi Feng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dongdong Yuan: Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qinggeng Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Huiping Deng: Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaofeng Xie: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFE0110400), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41907303, 52072387) and Shanghai Commission of Science and Technology Program (20DZ1204100).
